# Transcrição: 09- Beginnings

- **Fonte:** 09- Beginnings
- **Duração:** 13:53
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

Okay, you have your thesis statement. I told you that when we start telling stories, we always begin with the end. We start with that five-second moment, that moment of transformation or realization. Now that we know what it is, we're going to go all the way back to the beginning, because when we craft our stories, we start at the beginning. The beginning is one of the most precious locations for any story. The problem we have in life is we just assume people want to listen to us. So we don't put a lot of thought into how we start our stories. We assume attention when attention should never be assumed. Nobody cares about you. That's not true, but if you think that when you're working on storytelling, it's going to help you immensely. That means that the beginning of a story is the most precious space in a story. It's the opportunity to promise your audience that you know what you're doing and time will be well spent. If you've ever flown on a plane and you probably have, and you're afraid at all, you know that the two moments you're most afraid of is the takeoff and the landing. Unless you hit turbulence while you're in the middle of the air, it's oftentimes easy to forget you're even in the air. Once a plane's in the sky, it's kind of like a bus, but takeoff and landing, that's where people get nervous, the same thing in storytelling. The beginning of the story is very important because it's when your audience is nervous. They're wondering if the person who's speaking has something to say and whether they know how to say it. It is the promise that time will be well spent. The end of the story, which we'll talk about in the next video, is the fulfillment of that promise. It's the guarantee that you spent time with me and now I promise you it was worth something. If you nail the beginning in the end, the middle can often be messy and people will forget. You can make lots of mistakes in the middle if the beginning and the end are strong. So let's talk about the beginning. Some of the things I have to go into the beginning of a story. Two words I want you to remember. You can make these two words a habit that you use for the rest of your life. Two things that the beginning of every story should have. The first one is location. Location is an enormously powerful tool in storytelling. Location activates the mind's eye. Location begins that film running in the mind's eye and the reason is simple. If I say I'm standing in a bathroom, you now have pictured me standing in a bathroom. It could be the bathroom in your own home. It might be your parents' bathroom. Maybe it's a bathroom you've seen in a television show you watch all the time. It doesn't matter to me. If the specificity of the bathroom is unrelated to my story, I allow you to put me in the bathroom of your choice and that's enormously valuable to me because that bathroom has come with at least a thousand adjectives that I never have to say. So when I say I'm standing in a bathroom, you can tell me if the floor is tile, linoleum,

## [03:04 - 06:04]

or wood. You can tell me the color of the toilet. You can tell me if there's a bidet attached to the toilet. You can tell me if there is a sink or a shower in the bathroom. You can tell me what the towels look like in the bathroom. You can tell me if the soap is squirt or if there's an actual bar of soap for me to wash my hands. You've already decided the layout of the bathroom. You've decided if this bathroom has a shower and a bathtub or not. And if it's not relevant to my story, I don't care. You've decided the colors. You've decided the size of the mirror. You've picked all of those things for me simply by me saying bathroom. Location is so powerful. It should be one of the first things out of our mouths is where we are because it activates the movie. It creates an enormous landscape upon which we can work. I'm working on a story right now about my grandmother. She lived next door to me when I was growing up and I was terrified of her. My story might begin something like this. My grandmother's name was Oda Lee Dix, which probably explains a lot about why she was who she was. She was both crooked and mind and body. She wore black every day of her life. And when my mother sent me up to grandma's house, I was terrified every time. Or I could tell it like this. I'm standing on the edge of my grandmother's vegetable garden. She's in the middle of the garden pulling weeds. My grandmother's name is Oda Lee Dix, which probably explains a lot about why she was who she was. She's both crooked and mind and body. She wears black every day of her life. And as I'm standing there looking at her, I am terrified. Did you see it in your mind's eye? Did you see the difference between the first and the second? The first version of the story was an essay about my grandmother. And life lesson, nobody has ever asked for an essay about someone's grandmother. The second one though is a story, and I've opened with location which activated your imagination. I think you saw my grandmother in the middle of the vegetable garden. Now I don't know what vegetable garden you imagined, enormous or small. I don't know if you imagined that there was corn growing in the back or sunflowers or carrots. I don't know what you imagined, but if it's not relevant to the story, I don't care. You did other things that are kind of amazing that you don't even realize you did. You picked the season. You decided whether the things in the garden were just starting to sprout, whether they were fully grown, or whether they had just been picked. You decided the month that the story takes place in, you decided the weather. Was it a cloudy day or a sunny day? You decided the time of day by the length of shadows. You placed sky and land in my story and created a horizon without even trying. All of those things and truly hundreds of others, you put all of those things into my story by me starting with location.

## [06:04 - 09:08]

People ask me all the time, Matt, how do you create such vivid stories in my mind? How do you create stories that I can so clearly see? I say, I don't use adjectives. I avoid them whenever possible. Rather than adjectives, I use good, hard nouns to which adjectives are already affixed. So my grandmother standing in a vegetable garden has a million adjectives already affixed. You assume them, you fill them in because the power of your imagination is always going to be more powerful than the words that I use. Also, if I try to assemble a vegetable garden for you, the amount of work that's going to take for me to describe it and for you to hold on to it is terrible. I don't need to describe it because you already have it in your brain. All I do is take it out of your brain and tell you to use it and you do. So we start with location. The second thing we start with is action because people want stories to get off their feet. They want them moving. Pay attention to the way movies begin. They never begin with exposition, long descriptions. They start with something happening. In the very first Star Wars movie, the first opening scene is a little spaceship is being shot at by a big spaceship. We know nothing else about these spaceships, but the story has already begun. Many of Alfred Hitchcock's stories start with two people chasing each other. We don't know who's the bad guy, who's the good guy, but we don't care because the story has begun. So we begin with action. I'm walking across an empty parking lot on an early Saturday morning looking at an enormous edifice of a building. Action and location. I'm standing in front of an enormous pile of leaves on the playground behind my school and it's moving and a hand thrusts out with a metal object in it. Action and location. It activates the imagination. And you can practice this and do it with every single story you tell for the rest of your life. The only thing you get from this course, this entire storytelling course, is the idea that I'm going to start every story for the rest of my life with location and action. You're probably going to be the best storyteller in your life because most people start stories poorly. They don't take advantage of the preciousness of the real estate at the beginning of a story. Here's the one thing I don't want you to do at the beginning of a story. I don't want you to begin with exposition. Exposition is the idea that I must explain stuff to you before I start telling you the story. That is a terrible way to tell a story, but it is the way that most people tell stories. Those are the stories that begin with, I grew up in a little town in a little state with one flashlight and a pharmacy on the corner and a candy store on the other side and a single main street and I don't want to hear your story anymore because nothing has happened.

## [09:08 - 12:09]

You're just describing a town. That doesn't mean the description of the town can't be woven into the story, but it should not be the first thing you say. Think about Apollo 13, the Tom Hanks movie about the doomed lunar mission to the moon. To understand how that movie works, to understand what's going to happen in that movie, you have to understand 1960s space travel. You have to understand how that spaceship works in order to understand what they're going to do once they're trapped in space. The movie does not begin with Tom Hanks on a black screen saying, good evening ladies and gentlemen, before we begin the movie, I thought I'd give you a little bit of insight into 1960 space travel. Let me teach you about how the spaceship that you're going to see in the movie works. That would be a terrible way to start that movie. Instead, the movie starts and as it moves along, they teach us what we need to know as we move along. That's what you have to do in your stories. We want to avoid exposition completely. We want the story to start. We want it to get going. Pay attention to the way movies begin. Pay attention to the way directors begin films. It is always going to be something is happening. We want to be swept up into the story. The last bit of advice and maybe the most important bit of advice doesn't come from me. It comes from the author, Kurt Vonnegut. Kurt Vonnegut said that when you're writing a short story, and I believe any story, start as close to the end as possible. Pushing your story as close to the end as possible does a bunch of things. The first thing it does is it shortens your story and the shorter story is always the best version of your story. It also heightens drama and suspense by moving everything that you would have said that was unnecessary out of the way. In my bad blood draw story, I could have started the story with I wake up in the morning having to go to a blood draw. I get out of bed. Can you feel how that is terrible? That is a horrible way to begin that story. I start as close to the end as possible, which is I'm walking across the parking lot into the building. I feel like that's as close as I can get to still provide enough context so you understand the story. Think about the spoon of power. That story actually begins with the spoon of power. A metal object emerges from a pile of leaves. That's as close as I can get to the end of that story, the emergence of the spoon of power. I could have started the story with it's time for recess. I tell my students to go outside. I tell them I'll be right behind you. But no, why start as close to the end as possible? When I work with people on their stories, the number one change I make is I constantly move their beginning of the story closer to the end. So do that for yourself. Start as close to the end as possible. My favorite example of this is the Ocean's Eleven movies.

## [12:09 - 13:53]

These are movies about the robbing of a casino. If you and I were going to go rob a casino, we would probably spend 18 months, maybe two years, planning that robbery, but not in those movies. In those movies, we only get to see one or two days before the robbery. The planning of the robbery takes place in record time. Why? Because if I said to you, hey, let's go watch a movie. They're going to rob a casino, but we do have to watch the previous 18 months first to see how they planned it. We would not want to see that movie because that's bad storytelling. And so with all stories that we tell, we want to start as close to the end as possible. We want to push our beginnings forward, make our stories shorter, pack our stories with all of the suspense and excitement and drama and humor that we can in the smallest space available. That is what people want when they watch a story. If I asked you to go see a movie with me and I told you both movies are A plus level movies. One is five hours long. One is two hours long. Which movie are you choosing? The five hour movie actually gives you better bang for your buck. You get more for your dollar. A plus entertainment for five hours for the same price as the two hour movie. But which one are you choosing? You choosing the two hour movie because that's what people want. They want you to take the five hour movie and pack it into the two hour movie. By pushing your story as close to the end as possible, that is what you can achieve. So the beginning of your story, location and action. Avoid exposition and push your story as close to the ending as possible. Do those things and you will convince an audience that what you have to say is worth hearing.
